segments.s575-g49: فدای تو بادا is an optative formula: فدای (here an expression of self‑sacrifice) + تو (object 'you') + بادا (old optative particle 'may'). The whole chunk functions as a wish 'May I (or all sacrifices) be for you / May all be sacrificed to you.' همه خواسته follows as the noun phrase 'all desires' that are being wished to be sacrificed.
segments.s575-g50: کزین = 'from this / hence'; کین 'hatred, vengeance' is the object of that prepositional idea. همی is the archaic/progressive particle (here an emphatic/continuative element placed before the verb phrase). جان 'life' is the subject/affected thing, شود is the present/subjunctive 'become' (here with optative force: 'may be'), and کاسته is a past-participial/adjectival form meaning 'diminished/abated' — together 'may life become diminished/abated from this hatred.'
segments.s575-g51: تو دل‌خوش کن is an imperative-address: تو 'you' + دل‌خوش 'comforted / content' + کن imperative 'make / be' = 'be comforted' or 'take heart.' و links to the second command: شهر چندین (object phrase 'many a city' / 'several cities') followed by مسوز, the negative imperative 'do not burn' (lit. 'do not burn [the] many cities'). Word order places the object before the imperative for poetic effect.
segments.s575-g52: نباید is an impersonal modal 'it ought not / it should not'; که introduces the subordinate clause 'that ...'. روز ('day') is the grammatical subject, اندر آید is the verb phrase 'comes in / comes upon' (آید = present/subjunctive 'come'), and به‌روز at the end is an adverbial/phrase 'day by day / from day to day' placed for rhythmic emphasis. The clause expresses that it is improper that days should keep coming (i.e. further misfortune).
segments.s575-g53: نباشد is the negative form of باشد used impersonally here ('it is not' / 'it would not be'); پسند 'approval/pleasure' is the nominal predicate and جهان‌آفرین 'world‑creator' (genitive/agent) marks whose approval is meant. Together this gives 'It would not be pleasing to the Creator (i.e. the Creator would not approve).' Note the poetic terseness: the verb 'to be pleasing' is compacted into this negative impersonal form.
segments.s575-g54: که introduces the content clause 'that ...'. بیداد 'injustice/oppression' is the thing at issue; جوید is the present form of جوستن 'to seek' (here 'would seek' or 'seeks'); جهاندار is the agent 'the sovereign / ruler' performing the action; and کین 'vengeance/hatred' is coordinated as another object or motive. The clause therefore says the ruler seeking injustice and vengeance would not be pleasing to the Creator.
segments.s575-g55: درود (here 'blessings' / 'greetings') + جهاندار ('the sovereign') + بر ('upon') + شاه ('king') + باد (optative 'may'). This is a conventional benediction: 'May the sovereign's blessing be upon the king.' باد is the optative particle forming the wish.
segments.s575-g56: This is a parallel optative clause: بلند 'lofty' qualifies اخترش 'his star' (اختر + 3rd‑person possessive -ش). افسر is here a martial image ('helmet' or diadem) and ماه 'moon' (a poetic epithet for a beautiful or luminous sign); باد again is the optative 'may.' The phrase literally wishes 'May his lofty star be a helmet, (may) his moon (be so),' using celestial metaphors for honor/protection.
segments.s575-g73: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when' used with the past verb دادم. زنهار دادم is a simple past clause 'I warned' (زنهار = 'warning/admonition'; دادم = 'I gave' = 'I warned'). نسازمت is an archaic/poetic negative verbal form: ن‑ (negation) + ساز (root of ساختن/سازیدن 'to make/agree') + م (1st‑person) + ـت (2nd‑person object clitic), literally 'I did not make/fit you' but idiomatically here 'you would not consent/you did not comply' (the object -ت refers to 'you'); جنگ is the noun 'war/battle' and is the semantic result/threat linked to the previous clause.
segments.s575-g74: گشاده کنم is a verbal construction meaning 'I will open / I will make open' (گشاده 'open' + کردن → کنَم 'I do/make' in the present/subjunctive used for future/intent). بر تو is a prepositional phrase 'against you' (بر = 'against/upon', تو = 'you'). این راه تنگ is the direct object 'this narrow path' (این 'this' + راه 'path' + تنگ 'narrow'); note the adjective تنگ follows the noun as usual in Persian. The overall order is Adjective+کردن + prepositional target + noun phrase at the end, giving 'I will open/raise (an) open war against you and make this path narrow for you.'
segments.s575-g75: فرستاده is the agent noun 'the envoy/messenger' (used as subject). برگشت is the simple past 'returned'. و is the coordinating conjunction 'and'. پاسخ is the object 'the answer/reply'. ببرد here is a narrative past form of بردن ('to take/carry') meaning 'he carried (it) off/brought (it back)'; the subject is understood from context (the envoy). The clause reads 'The envoy returned and brought the reply.'
segments.s575-g76: سخنها is the plural object 'the words/sayings/speeches' (سخن + ها). یکایک is an adverb 'one by one' placed before the verb for emphasis. همه is an intensifier 'all/everyone' reinforcing totality. برشمرد is a past transitive verb 'he enumerated/recounted'. The order (object – adverb/emphatic – verb) is common in Persian narrative: 'He recounted all the words one by one.'
segments.s575-g1: یکی مرد بود is an existential/past clause: یکی 'a certain' + مرد 'man' + بود past copula 'was' = 'there was a man.' The following prepositional/genitive phrase از نژاد سران ('from/of the lineage of the chiefs') modifies مرد, with از + نژاد indicating 'of the lineage/stock' and سران (plural) 'the chiefs/heads.'
segments.s575-g2: هم here functions as 'also' or 'too', linking this phrase to the previous description. از تخمه نامور قیصران is another genitive phrase: تخمه 'offspring/stock' modified by the adjective نامور 'renowned', with قیصران 'the Caesars/emperors' as the source; the whole means 'also of the renowned seed/stock of the Caesars.'
segments.s575-g3: This is a verbless/nominal predicate with the final copula بود: برانوش نام = 'Baranush (was) the name' i.e. 'his name was Baranush.' The conjunction و links a second predicate خردمند 'wise,' so the structure is '[He/He whose name was Baranush] was wise.' The past copula بود places both predicates in the past.
segments.s575-g4: زبان و روانش is a coordinated nominal phrase 'his tongue (speech) and his soul' (روان + bound pronoun -ش = 'his'). پر از بند بود is the predicate: پر از 'full of' + بند 'stanzas/lines' (poetic sense), with بود the past copula. Word order places the possessed nouns before the predicate 'full of ... was.'
segments.s575-g5: بدو گفت is 'he said to him' (بدو = 'to him' as an enclitic/prepositional pronoun, گفت = 'said'). What follows is reported/optative content introduced by که ('that'): the clause قیصر تو باش is an optative/imperative-like construction 'you be Caesar' (باش is the present/imperative form 'be'). لشکر placed before که highlights 'the army' as the domain/context: 'that (as for) the army, you be Caesar (over it).' The order is somewhat poetic but follows Persian patterns of topicalization.
segments.s575-g6: برین is the preposition/particle بر + demonstrative ین (i.e. 'on/over that'), so برین لشکر = 'that army' or 'over that army.' و بوم ('and the land') is coordinated with لشکر. مهتر تو باش repeats the optative/imperative addressing 'you' (تو) with مهتر 'chief/leader' as predicative; باش is again the imperative/subjunctive 'be.' Overall: 'Be chief of that army and land.'
segments.s575-g7: به گفتار تو = 'to/for your speech' (به marking the indirect object/target), گوش دارد سپاه = 'the army has ear/attention' i.e. 'the army listens/pays attention.' Note the verb دارد (present 'has') with گوش 'ear/attention' expresses 'listens to' or 'attends to.' The subject سپاه follows the verb phrase for emphasis.
segments.s575-g8: This line gives two imperatival commands/requests in sequence: بیفروز (an imperative from a verb meaning 'make bright/illuminate' or 'set in splendor') with direct object تاج 'crown', and بیارای (imperative 'adorn') with object گاه (here 'place/seat/throne'). The conjunction و links the two objects/commands. Grammatically these are verb (imperative) + object phrases.
segments.s575-g25: This is an optative clause: the particle باد at the end is the classical Persian 'may' (wishing). جاوید (eternal) qualifies تاج (crown), and the possessive pronoun تو follows the noun (typical Persian post‑nominal possessive), so جاوید تاج تو = 'your everlasting crown.' The clause word order is poetic but functions as 'May your crown be everlasting.'
segments.s575-g26: Another optative clause ending in باد ('may'): همه (all/everyone) quantifies مهتران (great ones/chiefs). پیش تو (in your presence / before you) gives the location/relationship, and بنده (servant) is predicative: 'may all the chiefs be servants before you.' Note the adjective/nominal forms are used in a fixed polite/blessing formula.
segments.s575-g27: تو دانی is present tense 'you know'; که introduces the subordinate clause. تاراج و خون ریختن consists of two masdars/verbal nouns joined by و (pillage and the spilling of blood) and functions as the content of what 'you know.' ریختن here is used as a verbal noun meaning 'shedding/spilling.'
segments.s575-g28: چه introduces a manner/degree ('how' / in what way) here, and با marks the target/agency (with/against) — با بیگنه مردم = 'against foreign/innocent peoples.' آویختن is an infinitive/verbal noun meaning 'to attach oneself / to fall upon, to cling to' (used figuratively as 'to assail' or 'to fasten on'); it continues the list of actions that are the object of دانی ('you know how ...').
segments.s575-g29: This is another optative/wishive expression: مهان سرافراز ('honored/proud guests') is the noun phrase; دارند شوم is a poetic phrasing expressing a wish that the speaker 'be possessed/attended by' such guests — شوم is subjunctive ('may I become / may I be'), while دارند ('have') indicates possession/attendance in a lightly idiomatic construction meaning 'may I have honored guests.'
segments.s575-g30: The correlative چه ... چه ... links two locations: 'whether ... or ...'. با here marks the locus ('in/at' in classical usage), so چه با شهر ایران چه با مرز روم = 'whether in the city of Iran or at the frontier of Rome.' The sequence lists two spheres where the wished‑for guests might be present.
segments.s575-g31: گر is the conditional 'if.' این کین ایرج = 'this vengeance/hatred of Iraj' (این + noun + genitival name). به دست is an agent/agency phrase 'by the hand (of...)' and از نخست means 'from the beginning/at first' (adverbial). Together they set up a conditional clause: 'If this vengeance on Iraj was at first by (the hand of)...'.
segments.s575-g32: This is the main clause: منوچهر is the subject, کرد is simple past 'did / performed,' and آن ('that') is the direct object referring back to 'this vengeance.' به مردی درست is an adverbial phrase of manner — به + مردی ('with manliness/manhood') modified by درست ('properly/rightly') — so 'Manuchehr did that rightly, in a manly manner.'
segments.s575-g9: بیاراستند is a past-tense, 3rd-person-plural verb meaning 'they adorned' (compound bi- + āraštan). The prepositional phrase beginning with از marks the material or source: از برش تخت عاج = 'from/with the carving of the ivory throne.' The noun phrase برش تخت عاج is a genitive/compound: 'the carving (برش) of the ivory (عاج) throne (تخت)'.
segments.s575-g10: برانوش بنشست is a simple past clause: proper name + بنشست (3rd-sg past of 'to sit/seat') = 'Baranush sat.' The following phrase بر سرش تاج is a prepositional + nominal phrase ('on her head, a crown'); ش is the possessive suffix 'her'. Persian often omits a copula or auxiliary here, so the noun phrase after the verb reads as 'a crown upon her head' (the crown is presented as placed on her head).
segments.s575-g11: بنشاندند is a transitive past-tense verb (3rd-person plural) 'they seated/installed.' The prepositional phrase به جای بزرگیش literally 'to/in the place of her greatness' (به جای + بزرگیش) means 'in a position befitting her greatness' — جای = place/position and the -ش suffix marks 'his/her.' The direct object (her) is understood from the context.
segments.s575-g12: همه رومیان is the subject 'all the Romans' (همه + رومیان). آفرین is an interjective/nominal exclamation 'Bravo!' and خواندند (3rd-pl past) here means 'they uttered/called out' — used idiomatically for shouting or acclaiming. Word order is subject + exclamation + verb.
segments.s575-g13: This is two coordinated past actions: برانوش بنشست ('Baranush sat') + و ('and') + اندیشه کرد. اندیشه is a noun meaning 'thought' used with the light verb کرد to make the verbal idiom 'اندیشه کرد' = 'she thought/pondered.' کرد is the past 3rd-singular here.
segments.s575-g14: ز روم و ز آوردگاه نبرد are two ablative/locative phrases introduced by poetic ز ('from/about/regarding'): 'about/from Rome and about/from the battlefield of battle.' آوردگاه نبرد is a compound meaning 'the field/place of battle' and functions as the object of اندیشه (what she thought about).
segments.s575-g15: بدانست is past 3rd-singular 'she/it knew.' کو را is an archaic interrogative/relative phrase ('which/who' or 'whom') with را marking it as definite/emphatic object. ز شاه بلند is an ablative/partitive phrase 'of/from the lofty/high king' (شاه بلند = 'lofty king'), modifying کو (i.e., 'which one of the high kings').
segments.s575-g16: ز روم و ز آویزش are ablative phrases 'from Rome and from his (آویزش).' آویزش here is a nominal that in context refers to the man's attachment/retinue or that which clings to him; ز ... آید گزند is a clause in which آید (3rd-singular present 'comes') governs گزند ('harm'). In reported narrative this present can be read as a potential/future: 'harm will/comes from ...'.
segments.s575-g41: نباید is an impersonal negative modal (‘must not / should not’). که introduces the subordinate clause. ویران شود is a passive/subjunctive construction: شود here marks ‘become / be (laid) ruined’ (passive sense). بوم روم is the noun phrase ‘homeland of Rum’ (بوم = homeland, modified by روم), with poetic word order placing the place after the verb.
segments.s575-g42: که here is causal/concessive ‘for / because / that’, and چون functions temporally/casually as ‘when / if / since’. روم دیگر نبودست is an older negative past (‘would be no longer / were not’), with نبودست (archaic past negative) marking non‑existence. بوم repeats ‘homeland’ for emphasis — overall: ‘for when Rum no longer existed, there would be no homeland.’
segments.s575-g43: وگر is the conditional ‘if’. غارت و کشتنت places two gerundial/noun ideas (‘plunder’ and ‘your killing’) as the subject. بود is the past copula here indicating a hypothetical/policy (‘were’), and رای is the noun ‘plan / counsel’ — the phrase = ‘if plunder and your killing were the plan.’
segments.s575-g44: همه روم = ‘all (of) Rome’ (همه before the noun = entire/whole). گشتند is past plural of گشتن here meaning ‘became / were rendered’. بی‌دست و پای is an idiomatic coordinate phrase (‘without hands and feet’) i.e. ‘powerless/crippled’; بی‑ is the privative prefix ‘without’. The structure describes the result of the conditional.
segments.s575-g45: زن و کودکانش is the coordinated subject ‘his wife and his children’ (زن here = wife in context). اسیر is predicative ‘captives’. تواند is a modal/possibility verb (‘can / may / might be’), giving a potential result: ‘may be taken captive.’
segments.s575-g46: جگر خسته = literally ‘weary liver’ used metaphorically for the heart/soul being exhausted. از + تیغ و تیر marks cause/origin ‘from / by sword and arrow’. توانـد repeats the modal possibility (‘may be / may become’). The clause says his heart could be exhausted by arms.
segments.s575-g47: گه آمد is an impersonal phrase ‘it sometimes happens’ (گه = sometimes / on occasions; آمد = occurs/happens). که introduces the subordinate clause. کمتر کنی is subjunctive/optative ‘you make less / you temper’, where کنی is 2nd‑person subjunctive of کردن. کین و خشم are coordinated nouns ‘vengeance and anger’ — together: ‘that you reduce vengeance and anger.’
segments.s575-g48: فرو is a directional/intensifying element ‘down / deeply’. خوابنی is an adjective/participial sense ‘sleepy / put to sleep’ (here ‘sink into sleep’). از گذشته (literally ‘from the past’) gives a causal/temporal flavor, and دو چشم ‘two eyes’ is the patient experiencing the sleep — word order is somewhat compressed/poetic (‘from the past [the] two eyes’), so the phrase means the two eyes become heavy with sleep because of what has passed.
segments.s575-g57: نویسنده is the subject 'the scribe/writer.' بنهاد is a past tense verb 'placed/set' (3rd person) and پس is an adverb 'then,' while خامه را is the definite object 'the pen' with را marking it. Word order is subject–verb–(adverb)–object, typical of classical Persian narrative.
segments.s575-g58: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when/as,' اندر is the locative 'in/within,' and نوشت is the past tense 'wrote.' آن کیی نامه را is the object 'that Kayanian letter' with آن 'that' and را marking definiteness; the clause gives the time/ circumstance of the action.
segments.s575-g59: نهادند is a plural past 'they placed/affixed.' پس is 'then.' مهر قیصر is a genitival phrase 'Caesar's seal' (mhr + name) and بروی functions as a prepositional/locative element 'upon/on' (poetic usage); the object (the letter) is understood from the previous clause. Note the verb agreement is plural, referring to the court officials.
segments.s575-g60: فرستاده is 'a messenger' (the person sent). بنهاد here repeats the past-action verb 'placed/sent' (they caused to be sent). زی شاه روی is an idiomatic locative: زی (an older/preclassical preposition) + شاه 'the king' + روی 'face/presence' = 'to the king's presence.' The whole segment describes dispatching the messenger to the king.
segments.s575-g61: بیامد is past 'he/they came/arrived.' خردمند is the subject 'the wise man.' و is the coordinating 'and.' نامه بداد is a verb phrase where بداد here functions as a past-tense verb 'gave/handed over' (poetic contraction), meaning 'he gave the letter.' Word order is subject–verb–and–object+verb.
segments.s575-g62: ز is the archaic/prepoetic 'from,' marking source (here 'from Caesar'). به is the preposition 'to/for,' introducing the recipient. شاپور فرخ نژاد is a compound proper name/epithet (Shapur Farrokh‑nizād), with فرخ ('fortunate') and نژاد ('lineage') forming the epithet.
segments.s575-g63: چو is the temporal 'when.' آن نامور 'that famed one' is the topical/subject phrase. نامه is the object 'the letter.' برخواندند is a past plural verb 'they read aloud' (برخواندن), often used for public or formal reading; the plural suggests attendants/court read or the action was performed aloud to an assembly.
segments.s575-g64: سخنهای is the plural possessive/partitive 'words/sayings.' نغزش is formed from نغز + the 3rd‑person suffix -ش meaning 'his/her/its pithy/delicate' (an epithet of the wording). برافشاندند is past plural 'they scattered/poured forth' (metaphorically 'they uttered/poured out'), so the clause says the assembly voiced his elegant/pithy expressions.
segments.s575-g33: تن سلم is a possessive juxtaposition (literally 'the body of Salm'); the ezâfe is omitted in this archaic/poetic line. زان = 'from that' (a prepositional/causal idea) links to کین 'that hatred'. کنون is an adverb 'now/at present'. خاک شد is a verbal phrase where خاک (here 'dust/ashes') is the complement and شد is the past/become verb: 'became dust'.
segments.s575-g34: هم = 'also/likewise' linking this clause to the previous one. از is the preposition 'from/out of'. تور here is the proper name Tūr (Tur), so it is the referent removed from the earth. روی زمین is a compound 'the face/surface of the earth' (روی = face/surface, زمین = earth). پاک شد = 'was made clean/cleared' (پاک 'clean, pure' + شد 'became/was').
segments.s575-g35: وگر is the conditional 'if'. کین is the noun 'this hatred/vengeance' (subject of the clause). دارا here functions as the proper name Dārā (Darius) and is followed by the archaic copula ست ('is'); the line therefore says the hatred is with Dārā and with Iskandar. و between دارا/ست and اسکندری is the simple coordinating 'and'.
segments.s575-g36: که introduces a relative clause modifying اسکندری ('Iskandar, who...'). نو = 'new' or 'fresh'. شد is the past/copular 'became/was made'. بر وی = 'upon him' (بر = upon; وی = third‑person pronoun 'him/it' used in classical diction). زمین داوری is a noun phrase 'the judgment/rule of the earth' (زمین modifies داوری), so the whole clause says the rule/judgment of the earth became new upon him (i.e. he newly assumed rulership).
segments.s575-g37: مر is a dative/benefactive 'for me' (an older form of modern 'مرا'/'برای من' in such usages). او را is the marked direct object 'him' (او + object marker را). دو دستور بد is the subject 'two bad/evil orders/decrees' (دستور = command/order, بد = bad). کشته بود is the past perfect/periphrastic 'had killed' (کشته past participle + بود auxiliary).
segments.s575-g38: و دیگر = 'and another/one more' continuing the enumeration. کزو = 'from which/from whom' (ک + زو = 'of/from it/him'), referring back to that other decree. بخت‌برگشته is a compound adjective: بخت 'fortune' + برگشته 'turned/returned' — here used idiomatically about a change in luck (rendered negative by context); بود is the past copula 'was'.
segments.s575-g39: گرت is a conditional particle 'if (it be that)'. کین قیصر = 'the hatred/vengeance of Caesar' (کین + proper name قیصر). فزاید is the verb 'increases' (3rd person singular present), and the enclitic همی is the archaic continuous/emphatic particle often used with verbs in epic diction (here reinforcing the ongoing/inclining sense: 'keeps increasing / should increase').
segments.s575-g40: به زندان = 'into/into the prison' (به + place noun). تو is the 2nd person pronoun 'you' (the one addressed). بند ساید همی is an older optative/passive construction: بند here evokes the idea of being fettered/bound, ساید expresses an optative/potential sense ('may it be' / 'may you be'), and همی again is the continuous/emphatic particle; the clause functions as a curse/wish: 'may you be bound in prison.'
segments.s575-g17: فرستاده‌ای is an indefinite/participle-like noun meaning 'a sent one / a messenger' (classical Persian often uses participial forms as nouns). جست is a past-tense verb here meaning 'sought' (classical past of جستن); the sequence 'Noun + past-verb' is common in older Persian narrative. بارای is an archaic poetic form of برای 'for, for the sake of', and شرم here means 'modesty' (a positive, courteous sense rather than only shame). Together the phrase means 'he sought a messenger for the sake of modesty.'
segments.s575-g18: که introduces a purpose/relative clause here ('so that / who'). دانش functions as the object/subject of سراید and means 'learning, knowledge' (scholarship). سراید is the present/subjunctive-like poetic form of سراییدن used here with the sense 'to sing/recite' (i.e. to utter or render in speech). به + آواز نرم is an instrumental phrase 'in/with a gentle voice' — به used adverbially ('in, by') and نرم as an adverb/adjective 'softly, gently.'
segments.s575-g19: دبیری is a noun form referring to the office/role of a secretary or the secretarial function (here used as 'a secretary' in context). The adjective بزرگ follows the noun (standard Persian order: noun + adjective). و connects the second adjective phrase جهاندیده‌ای ('worldly‑wise' with an indefinite/participle-like -ای). The -ای ending marks indefiniteness or descriptive participle in classical diction.
segments.s575-g20: خردمند and دانا are two coordinated adjectives meaning 'wise' and 'learned'; they describe the same person. پسندیده‌ای is a past/perfect (classical narrative) participial form of پسندیدن meaning 'chosen / approved' (here effectively 'he chose / he selected' or 'the preferred one'); the ‑ای ending is an archaic/classical morphological marker often used in Ferdowsi.
segments.s575-g21: بیاورد is a past-tense narrative form 'he brought (him)'. The conjunction و links the next verb بنشاند, a causative/past form meaning 'he made (him) sit / he seated (him)' — classical transitive use. نزدیک خویش is a prepositional/adverbial phrase 'near himself'; خویش + enclitic ش (here separated in the tokenization) is the reflexive/possessive 'his own / himself.' Verb sequence 'brought and seated' narrates two actions performed by the subject (implied 'he').
segments.s575-g22: بگفت is the past narrative 'he said' (used to introduce quoted or reported speech). آن سخنهای باریک خویش is the object phrase 'those / the subtle (باریک = delicate, fine, subtle) words of his' — آن is the demonstrative 'that/those', سخنهای is the plural 'words/speeches' and خویش is the possessive 'his'. Word order (verb then demonstrative + noun phrase) is typical of older Persian narrative.
segments.s575-g23: یکی here is an indefinite 'someone / a certain one'. نامه is 'a letter'. بنوشت is the classical past form meaning 'wrote' (نوشتن with the old prefix), but the lemma provided in the metadata does not reflect this verbal sense; in the poem it functions as the past tense 'he wrote'. پرآفرین is an adjectival phrase 'full of praise / of high praise' describing the letter.
segments.s575-g24: ز is the poetic/prepositional form 'from'. دادار is a common epithet for the Creator/God. بر here means 'to / toward' (directional 'to the king'). شهریار زمین is a genitival/noun phrase 'the king (lord) of the land' (شهریار = king; زمین = the land/earth). The whole segment gives the sender and recipient: 'from the Creator to the king of the land.'
segments.s575-g65: ببخشود is a past tense verb meaning 'he/she forgave' (classical/poetic past). The conjunction و links the two coordinated actions. دیده (literally 'the eye') is the definite/nominal object placed before the predicate; پر از آب کرد is a causative construction: پر 'full (of)' + از 'of/from' + آب 'water' and کرد 'made/did' (here 'caused to be'), so the phrase means 'caused the eye(s) to be full of water' (i.e. brought tears). Word order follows classical Persian: verb (ببخشود) — conjunction — nominal phrase — result clause with کرد as the light verb.
segments.s575-g66: This clause has an elided subject and a compound noun phrase 'بروهای جنگی' followed by the predicate 'پر از تاب کرد'. جنگی is an adjective/attributive 'warlike' or 'of war' modifying بروهای; پر از تاب + کرد is again a causative/light‑verb construction 'made full of تاب'. تاب here refers to 'sway/animation' (poetic sense: fluttering or motion), so the clause means 'he caused the war‑(things) to be full of motion' (naturally: 'the war‑standards/flags/armaments fluttered'). The morphology is classical: plural/possessive marker -های on برو (here a nominal form), and کرد as the past tense causative.
segments.s575-g67: هم‌اندر زمان is an adverbial time phrase meaning 'at the same time' or 'meanwhile' (هم‌اندر lit. 'also/at the same time + time'). نامه پاسخ نوشت follows classical Persian word order: direct object(s) (نامه 'the letter', پاسخ 'an answer') precede the verb نوشت 'he wrote'. نوشت is simple past 'wrote'.
segments.s575-g68: بگفت is a past narrative variant meaning 'he said' (بـ- prefix appears in older/poetic forms). آنکجا رفته is a locative/relative phrase: آنکجا 'there/where' + رفته (past participle 'gone') = 'where you have gone' or 'to that place which is gone to'. The sequence بد خوب و زشت is an asyndetic enumerative predicate (no copula) listing adjectives 'bad, good, and ugly' describing what is found there; the list order is stylistic and elliptical, typical in poetry.
segments.s575-g69: The clause begins with the relative particle که introducing an example or relative clause. مهمان is the subject 'the guest'. The prepositional phrase به چرم خر اندر (lit. 'to/into the donkey's leather inside') precedes the past verb دوخت 'sewed/stitched' — classical word order puts objects/prepositional phrases before the verb. This construction therefore means roughly 'the guest who sewed (it) into the donkey's leather' (poetic/elliptical; some objects are omitted and must be inferred).
segments.s575-g70: Another relative clause introduced by که. بازار کین کهن is a noun phrase 'the market of ancient kīn (vengeance/hatred)' where کین کهن is a genitive/attributive group ('old/ancient grudge'). The verb برفروخت is a classical past form meaning 'sold' (بر- prefix + فروخت). The clause reads 'who sold the old market of vengeance' or more idiomatically 'who sold (or traded in) ancient hatred' — word order places the noun phrase before the past verb, standard in Ferdowsi's style.
segments.s575-g71: This is an address + imperative sequence. تو (you) is the vocative/subject; گرد بخردی is an epithet meaning roughly 'surrounded by/possessing wisdom' or 'rich in wisdom' (گرد used poetically as an attributive element with بخردی 'wisdom'), an archaic/poetic formation. خیز is an imperative 'rise!' and پیش من آی is a second imperative 'come before me' (پیش 'before/in the presence of' + من 'me' + آی 'come!'). The imperatives are direct commands addressed to the addressee.
segments.s575-g72: خود functions as an emphatic pronoun 'yourself' or 'you yourself' and is linked by و 'and' to the following noun phrase. فیلسوفان پاکیزه رای is a genitive/attributive phrase 'philosophers of clean/pure counsel' (پاکیزه qualifies رای 'opinion/counsel'), so the full string is an appositive/coordinate: 'you yourself and the philosophers of pure counsel' — i.e. the people he is summoning.

sentences.s575-s25: May all desires be sacrificed for you, so that from this hatred my life may be diminished.
sentences.s575-s26: Take heart, and do not burn many a city; it should not be that day after day comes.
sentences.s575-s27: It is not pleasing to the world‑creator that the sovereign should seek injustice and vengeance.
sentences.s575-s28: Blessings of the sovereign be upon the king; may his lofty star be a helmet, and his moon (be) a crest.
sentences.s575-s37: When I warned you and you would not heed, I will wage open war against you and make this path narrow for you.
sentences.s575-s38: The envoy returned and carried back the reply; he recounted all the words one by one.
sentences.s575-s1: There was a man of the chiefs' lineage, also sprung from the famed stock of the Caesars.
sentences.s575-s2: His name was Baranush and he was wise; his speech and his soul were full of verse.
sentences.s575-s3: He said to him, 'Let the army be yours — be Caesar; be the chief of that army and land.'
sentences.s575-s4: The army pays heed to your words; (so) illuminate the crown and adorn the throne/place.
sentences.s575-s13: May your crown be everlasting, and may all the great ones be your servants before you.
sentences.s575-s14: You know that pillage and the shedding of blood, and how one falls upon foreign peoples.
sentences.s575-s15: May I be attended by honored guests, whether in the city of Iran or on the frontier of Rome.
sentences.s575-s16: If this vengeance against Iraj was at first by (the) hand (of someone), Manuchehr did that rightly and in a manly way.
sentences.s575-s5: They adorned the throne with ivory carving; Baranush sat, a crown upon her head.
sentences.s575-s6: They seated her in a place befitting her greatness; all the Romans shouted 'Bravo!'
sentences.s575-s7: Baranush sat and pondered on Rome and the battlefield of war.
sentences.s575-s8: She knew which one of the lofty kings — from Rome and from his followers — harm would come.
sentences.s575-s21: The homeland of Rum must not be laid waste, for if Rum were gone there would be no homeland left.
sentences.s575-s22: If pillage and your slaughter were the plan, all of Rome would be left powerless.
sentences.s575-s23: His wife and children may be taken captive; his heart may be worn out by sword and arrow.
sentences.s575-s24: Sometimes it happens that you lessen your vengeance and anger, and your two eyes sink into sleep over the past.
sentences.s575-s29: The scribe then set his pen down as he wrote that Kayanian letter.
sentences.s575-s30: They then affixed Caesar's seal upon it and sent a messenger to the king's presence.
sentences.s575-s31: The wise man arrived and handed the letter from Caesar to Shapur Farrokh‑nizād.
sentences.s575-s32: When that famed man read the letter aloud, they poured forth his pithy words.
sentences.s575-s17: Salm's body, from that hatred, has now turned to dust; likewise Tur's presence on the face of the earth was cleared away.
sentences.s575-s18: And if this hatred rests with Dārā and with Iskandar — Iskandar, upon whom the rulership of the earth became new.
sentences.s575-s19: For me, two wicked decrees had slain him, and another (one) from which his fortune had turned (to ill).
sentences.s575-s20: If Caesar's hatred increases, then may you be bound in prison.
sentences.s575-s9: He sought a messenger for the sake of modesty and propriety, so that he might recite learning in a gentle voice.
sentences.s575-s10: He selected a great, worldly‑wise secretary — a wise and learned man.
sentences.s575-s11: He brought him and set him down near himself, and spoke those subtle words of his.
sentences.s575-s12: Someone wrote a letter full of praise — from the Creator to the king of the land.
sentences.s575-s33: He pardoned him and his eyes filled with water; he made the war‑standards full of motion.
sentences.s575-s34: At the same time he wrote a reply to the letter and said, 'There where you have gone are the bad, the good, and the ugly.'
sentences.s575-s35: — (one) who, as guest, stitched (it) into the donkey's leather; (one) who sold the ancient market of vengeance.
sentences.s575-s36: You, rich in wisdom, rise — come before me, you yourself and the philosophers of pure counsel.
